content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

October 16, 1996

Rachel G. Clark
City Clerk
City of San Bernardino
300 North D Street
Post Office Box 1318
San Bernardino, California 92402

Jerry Devlin
Councilmember
City of San Bernardino
300 North D Street
Post Office Box 1318
San Bernardino, California 92402

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-276

Dear Ms. Clark and Mr. Devlin:

This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

QUESTION

May you use campaign funds to pay for a city employee of your
choice to participate in a nine-month leadership class?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The expenditure is directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. Keep in mind, however, that
if the employee you plan to send is a designated employee, the
tuition payment may be subject to the $280 gift limit. Further,

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations
appear at Title 2, Sections 18000-18995 of the California Code of
Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-96-276
Page 2


this advice is prospective only and may not be construed to apply
to any past conduct.

FACTS

Leadership San Bernardino is a nine-month program designed to
promote the development of leadership skills and community
awareness of San Bernardino's future leaders. Candidates for the
program were sought from all racial, ethnic, cultural, business,
community and governmental backgrounds. In the Leadership San
Bernardino Committee's attempts to find candidates for the
program, a recent speaker at the Rotary Breakfast Club, which you
belong to, explained the Leadership San Bernardino Program and
sought recommendations from your club's membership on possible
candidates for the program and encouraged sponsors for tuition
costs.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act's "personal use" provisions govern
the use of campaign funds under the general theory that campaign
funds are held in trust for the purpose of seeking and holding
office. (Section 89510(a).) Section 89513(f)(1) specifies that
campaign funds may not be used to make personal gifts unless the
gift is directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. A gift of campaign funds worth $250 or less
to an employee of an official's agency is statutorily considered
to be directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. (Section 89513(f)(2).)

A gift is defined as "any payment to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value
unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of
business to members of the public without regard to official
status." (Section 82028.) Designated employees of a local
government agency are subject to a gift limit of $280 from any
source required to be reported on the employee's statement of
economic interests. (Section 89503(c); Regulation 18940.1.)

You plan to pay the tuition of an individual city employee to
attend the Leadership San Bernardino program. You nominated the
employee for the program and agreed to split the cost of her $775
tuition fees. Each campaign committee would pay approximately
$332 in fees. These tuition payments would be a personal gift
from your respective campaign committees to the employee.2

_______________
2 This case is unlike the Ayala Advice Letter, No. A-95-017, in
which we advised that it was a permissible use of campaign funds
for a candidate to donate $50,000 to an educational institution
for a scholarship fund. In the Ayala letter, the funds were
donated directly to the school and dispensed by the school, rather
than by the candidate.

File No. A-96-276
Page 3




     As noted above, Section 89513(f)(2) provides that a gift
worth $250 or less given to an employee of the candidate's agency
is deemed to be directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. To the extent each of you will be donating
over $250, those excess funds would be subject to the provisions
of Section 89513(f)(1) and may not be donated unless the gift is
directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose. Examples of gifts directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose include returning a campaign
contribution to individuals who were defrauded by the original
donor of the contribution, a retirement party for an officeholder,
and meals provided to attendees of a conference by a committee
that cosponsored the conference. (Bergeson Advice Letter, No. A-
95-212; McDonald Advice Letter, No. A-94-240; and Farley Advice
Letter, No. A-92-299.)

     You stated that the purpose of the gift of tuition is to
develop potential leadership skills, and you chose a city employee
so that the city would ultimately reap the benefits of having a
participant enrolled in the program. Therefore, it appears that
the gift of tuition is directly related to a governmental purpose.
If the employee is a designated employee of the city, however, she
may not accept a gift of over $280 from a single source in a
calendar year. You have not requested advice regarding the
employee's obligations, nor provided us with facts indicating
whether the employee is designated in her agency's conflict-of-
interest code.

     If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5660.


                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Liane Randolph
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LR:ak
